_theta_method: theta_deg values for the five real flybys (Earth/launch, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune) are each planet's own heliocentric ecliptic mean longitude on the encounter date — since a flyby puts the probe essentially at the planet's position. Computed from J2000.0 mean-longitude orbital elements (E. M. Standish, JPL, 'Keplerian Elements for Approximate Positions of the Major Planets', table valid 1800-2050 AD: https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/planets/approx_pos.html) as theta = (L0 + Ldot*T) mod 360, where T is centuries since J2000.0 (JD 2451545.0) and L0/Ldot are each planet's tabulated mean longitude at epoch and rate. This ignores the equation-of-center (eccentricity) correction, so it is a first-order mean-longitude approximation, not a full ephemeris — adequate for a schematic top-down orrery, not for precision astronomy. r_au values are the well-known approximate flyby distances from the Sun. Cruise-phase points (is_flyby:false, seq 2,4,6,7,9,11,12) are NOT ephemeris positions: they are linear interpolations of r_au and heliocentric angle (shortest arc) between the two neighbouring real waypoints, by time fraction, added only so the drawn trajectory bends smoothly between flybys. The Heliopause point (seq 13) reuses Neptune's theta_deg: after the Neptune encounter Voyager 2 flew a ballistic, nearly radial outward path with no further gravity assists, so its heliocentric longitude drifted only slightly over the following 29 years — holding Neptune's angle is a deliberate, flagged approximation, not a computed one.

navigator.name: Voyager 2
navigator.bio: Voyager 2 launched from Cape Canaveral on 20 August 1977, sixteen days before its twin Voyager 1, on a trajectory timed to exploit a rare once-every-176-years alignment of the four giant planets — the 'Planetary Grand Tour'. It remains the only spacecraft ever to fly by all four: Jupiter (1979), Saturn (1981), Uranus (1986) and Neptune (1989), using each planet's gravity to bend and accelerate it on to the next, and returning the only close-up images of the two ice giants humanity has taken. On 5 November 2018 it crossed the heliopause into interstellar space, following Voyager 1. Both probes carry the Golden Record, a phonograph disc of Earth's sounds, images and languages, and both are still transmitting from beyond the Sun's influence, powered by slowly decaying plutonium-238.

voyage.title: Voyager 2: The Grand Tour of the Giant Planets (1977– )
voyage.purpose: Exploit a rare 176-year alignment of Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus and Neptune to fly a single probe past all four giant planets, using each encounter's gravity assist to bend and accelerate the spacecraft on to the next — a mission plan proposed as the 'Planetary Grand Tour'.
voyage.summary: Launched 20 August 1977 on a Titan IIIE-Centaur, Voyager 2 used gravity assists at Jupiter (July 1979) and Saturn (August 1981) to bend its path on to Uranus (January 1986) and Neptune (August 1989) — the only spacecraft ever to visit either ice giant. It returned the first, and so far only, close-up images of their rings, atmospheres and moon systems, discovering new moons and rings at each stop. It continued outward past Neptune and crossed the heliopause into interstellar space on 5 November 2018. More than four and a half decades after launch, it is still operating and still transmitting.

waypoints.date_note: Launched from Cape Canaveral Air Force Station, Florida, on a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket. theta_deg is Earth's own J2000 mean-longitude position on the launch date (see _theta_method).
waypoints.event: Voyager 2 lifts off atop a Titan IIIE-Centaur, sixteen days before its twin Voyager 1, aimed at the rare once-every-176-years alignment of the four giant planets that makes a single-probe grand tour possible.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: Launch mass 825 kg, carrying eleven scientific instruments and the Golden Record — a copper phonograph disc bearing sounds, images and greetings from Earth, intended to outlast the Sun.

waypoints.date_note: Not a NASA-published data point: r_au and theta_deg are linearly interpolated between the Earth and Jupiter waypoints by time fraction, added only to bend the drawn trajectory. See _theta_method.
waypoints.event: Deep-space cruise phase, crossing the asteroid belt on the way to Jupiter.

waypoints.date_note: Closest approach 9 July 1979, about 570,000 km above the cloud tops. theta_deg is Jupiter's own J2000 mean-longitude position on the encounter date (see _theta_method).
waypoints.event: Closest approach to Jupiter. Voyager 2 studies the Great Red Spot, the faint Jovian ring (discovered by Voyager 1 months earlier), and the volcanic moon Io, then uses Jupiter's gravity to bend and accelerate on toward Saturn.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: A colour composite of the Great Red Spot, taken 29 June 1979 from over 9 million km out, shows structure within the storm and a white oval drifting below it — the smallest features resolved are about 170 km across.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: NASA/JPL Photojournal, PIA01370 — "Jupiter's Great Red Spot"

media.caption: Jupiter's Great Red Spot, imaged by Voyager 2, 29 June 1979

waypoints.date_note: Not a NASA-published data point: r_au and theta_deg are linearly interpolated between the Jupiter and Saturn waypoints by time fraction, added only to bend the drawn trajectory. See _theta_method.
waypoints.event: Deep-space cruise phase, outbound from Jupiter toward Saturn.

waypoints.date_note: Closest approach 26 August 1981, about 101,000 km above the cloud tops. theta_deg is Saturn's own J2000 mean-longitude position on the encounter date (see _theta_method).
waypoints.event: Closest approach to Saturn. Voyager 2 returns high-resolution views of the rings — including the mysterious dark 'spokes' in the B-ring — and the moon system, then bends onward toward Uranus. A scan-platform stall shortly after encounter briefly interrupted imaging.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: A true-colour view acquired 21 July 1981 from about 33.9 million km shows Saturn with the moons Rhea and Dione against the rings, cloud bands already resolved in the southern hemisphere.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: NASA/JPL Photojournal, PIA00030 — "Saturn With Rhea and Dione (True Color)"

media.caption: Saturn with Rhea and Dione, true color, Voyager 2, 21 July 1981

waypoints.date_note: Not a NASA-published data point: r_au and theta_deg are linearly interpolated between the Saturn and Uranus waypoints by time fraction, added only to bend the drawn trajectory. See _theta_method.
waypoints.event: Deep-space cruise phase on the long leg out to Uranus.

waypoints.date_note: Not a NASA-published data point: r_au and theta_deg are linearly interpolated between the Saturn and Uranus waypoints by time fraction, added only to bend the drawn trajectory. See _theta_method.
waypoints.event: Deep-space cruise phase, still inbound toward Uranus.

waypoints.date_note: Closest approach 24 January 1986, about 81,500 km above the cloud tops. theta_deg is Uranus's own J2000 mean-longitude position on the encounter date (see _theta_method).
waypoints.event: Closest approach to Uranus — the only spacecraft ever to visit it. Voyager 2 discovers ten new moons and two new rings, and finds a magnetic field tilted almost 60 degrees from the planet's spin axis. Uranus itself, tipped on its side, shows an almost featureless pale blue-green disc.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: Narrow-angle camera frames from the January 1986 encounter, combined into true- and false-colour views, show a hazy, nearly blank disc — far less atmospheric structure than Jupiter or Saturn had shown.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: NASA/JPL Photojournal, PIA18182 — "Uranus as seen by NASA's Voyager 2"

media.caption: Uranus as seen by Voyager 2, 1986 encounter

waypoints.date_note: Not a NASA-published data point: r_au and theta_deg are linearly interpolated between the Uranus and Neptune waypoints by time fraction, added only to bend the drawn trajectory. See _theta_method.
waypoints.event: Deep-space cruise phase, outbound from Uranus toward the final planetary encounter at Neptune.

waypoints.date_note: Closest approach 25 August 1989, about 4,950 km above the cloud tops — the closest of any planetary flyby in the mission. theta_deg is Neptune's own J2000 mean-longitude position on the encounter date (see _theta_method).
waypoints.event: Closest approach to Neptune, the last of the four giant planets and the last planetary encounter of the primary mission. Voyager 2 finds the Great Dark Spot, supersonic winds, six new moons, faint rings, and flies close past the moon Triton before heading out of the plane of the planets toward interstellar space.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: A contrast-enhanced colour view from 14 August 1989, 14.8 million km out, shows bright wispy clouds overlying the Great Dark Spot's southern and northwestern margins.

media.caption: Neptune, color contrast enhanced, Voyager 2, 14 August 1989

waypoints.date_note: Not a NASA-published data point: r_au is linearly interpolated between the Neptune and Heliopause waypoints by time fraction; theta_deg holds Neptune's encounter angle (see _theta_method — Voyager 2's post-Neptune path is ballistic and nearly radial, so angular drift is small).
waypoints.event: Extended mission: the Voyager Interstellar Mission. Instruments keep sampling the outer heliosphere as the probe climbs toward the edge of the Sun's influence.

waypoints.date_note: Not a NASA-published data point: r_au is linearly interpolated between the Neptune and Heliopause waypoints by time fraction; theta_deg holds Neptune's encounter angle (see _theta_method).
waypoints.event: Still climbing outward through the heliosheath, the turbulent outer boundary region where the solar wind slows as it presses against the interstellar medium.

waypoints.date_note: Crossing date and ~119 AU distance are NASA-published mission facts. theta_deg is an approximation, not computed: it holds Neptune's encounter angle since the post-Neptune trajectory is ballistic and nearly radial (see _theta_method) — flagged rather than guessed.
waypoints.event: Voyager 2 crosses the heliopause, the boundary where the Sun's solar wind gives way to the interstellar medium, becoming the second human-made object (after Voyager 1 in 2012) to reach interstellar space. Instruments recorded a steep drop in solar-originating particles and a rise in galactic cosmic rays at the crossing.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: At the moment of crossing, Voyager 2 was about 18 billion km (11 billion miles) from Earth — over 119 AU — with a one-way radio signal taking more than 16.5 hours to arrive.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: NASA, "NASA's Voyager 2 Probe Enters Interstellar Space" (10 Dec 2018); NASA/JPL Photojournal PIA22835 — "Two Interstellar Travelers"

media.caption: Two Interstellar Travelers — positions of Voyager 1 and Voyager 2 beyond the heliosphere
